source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

Seven minutes. That is how long the average apology lasts. Not long enough. The apologizer speaks for six of those minutes. The apologized-to gets one. Sometimes less. Watch the hands. Apologizers gesture outward — open palms, arms wide. The other person crosses their arms. Folds inward. Nobody teaches us to receive an apology. Only to give one. That asymmetry is the real problem. Not the original offense.

Forgetting is faster than learning. It took her four years to memorize the periodic table. Three weeks to forget it. The forgetting curve has a name: Ebbinghaus. Nobody remembers that name. This is not an accident. The brain does not archive. It compresses. Lossy. Like a bad photograph of a good moment. You keep the feeling. Lose the detail. Ask her what element sits at 47. She'll pause. Silver. She knew that once without pausing.

Three chairs. Two of them match. The third is from a different set — you can tell by the legs. Nobody mentions it. Guests sit in whichever chair is nearest. The host always takes the odd one. Has for eleven years. Why? Not preference. Obligation. The odd chair must be occupied by someone. The host decided this without deciding it. One morning it was just true. Rules like this run most households. They are never written. Rarely spoken. Everyone obeys them anyway.

Nobody counts the steps they take in their own home. Estimate: four hundred, maybe five hundred per day. The kitchen receives the most. The corner near the window least. Floors remember this. Not literally — wood compresses unevenly, shows wear where feet have been. The path from bed to bathroom is always darker than the rest. Twelve years of the same route, same hour, same half-asleep shuffle. We call it comfort. What it is: a groove. The body is very good at grooves. The mind prefers to believe it chose each step freely.

Rain delays a decision by an average of two days. Not the task. The decision to begin the task. This has been measured. People wait for sun to sign contracts, start diets, call estranged relatives. The calendar is a prop. We use it to make procrastination look like planning. Monday is the most popular start date in history. Nothing has ever begun on a Thursday. Well — very little. Thursday belongs to continuation. The same is true of weather. Rain doesn't stop us. It postpones us. That is a different thing entirely. Ask someone why they didn't call. They'll blame the rain. They mean: I needed one more day to not be ready.

A hospital has one hundred and twelve clocks. Nobody counted. Someone estimated, then the estimate became fact. Clocks in hospitals do not tell the same time. They drift. Maintenance is scheduled. It is often skipped. The result: a six-minute window where, depending on which hallway you are standing in, a patient is either alive or not yet due for medication. Time in hospitals is not synchronized. It is negotiated. Every nurse knows which clock runs fast. They adjust mentally, automatically, the way a translator stops noticing they are translating. Nobody filed a report. Nobody will.

A library has a returns shelf. Books left there wait to be reshelved. Waiting period: usually two days. Sometimes a week. Nobody monitors the shelf officially. A volunteer does it, same volunteer, Tuesday mornings, has for six years. She knows which books came back damaged. She knows by feel. The spine tells her. She never marks them. The system says to mark them. She decides instead who will check them out next — someone young, someone who won't notice, someone who checks out three at a time and probably skims. She is not cruel. She is practical. The damaged books still circulate. No report exists. The system believes every book is fine.

Every office building has a floor that smells different from the others. Usually the third. No one reports this. It was always like this. The smell is old carpet, recycled air, and something chemical that facilities cannot name. Facilities tried once. The test came back inconclusive. The report was filed. The report was then misfiled. Nobody looks for it now. Eleven people work on that floor and none of them mention the smell to each other. Each assumes the others don't notice. Each notices. The smell becomes background. Background becomes invisible. This is not adaptation. It is agreement — silent, total, never formalized. The building continues to be inspected. The third floor always passes.

The second-to-last page of any instruction manual is the most read page. Not the first. Not the troubleshooting section. The second-to-last. Nobody has verified this. It sounds verified. People arrive there after giving up on the beginning and before accepting they are lost. The page often contains a warranty. Sometimes a diagram nobody needed three steps ago. The margin on that page is where most people write their name. Not for ownership. To prove they were present. Seventy-three percent of people who return a product have read exactly that page and no others. The number is invented. The behavior is not. Returned products are rarely broken. They are simply not understood. The manual absorbs the blame. The manual never complains.

Every train in the country runs four minutes late. Not three. Not five. Four. This was measured once, in 1987, by a graduate student who lost the data before publishing. The number survived. It spread through engineering departments, became a standard, got cited in reports that cite other reports. Nobody traced it back. The trains do not know they are late. The schedule was adjusted in 2003 to absorb the four minutes. The trains are now on time. The lateness is built into punctuality. Passengers still feel the delay. They arrive at the platform early, wait, watch the board. The board says: on time. The passengers are skeptical. The passengers are wrong. The system fixed the problem by redefining arrival. No announcement was made. None was needed.

A hotel housekeeper knocks twice before entering. Always twice. This is not policy. Policy says three. The third knock is for insurance, not courtesy. She skips it because the third knock woke a child once, in room 214, eleven years ago, and the mother complained, and the complaint was logged, and the log was never reviewed. She carries this memory. The hotel does not. She has knocked twice, every morning, for eleven years. Nobody has noticed the discrepancy. Nobody has complained. The rooms are always clean. The guests are always satisfied. Satisfaction scores have risen steadily since 2013. The housekeeper is not mentioned in the satisfaction scores. She is not mentioned anywhere. The hotel believes its policy is followed. The policy is not followed. Both things are true.

The coffee machine on the seventh floor has been broken since March. Not this March. A different March. The exact year is recorded on a Post-it note attached to the machine. The Post-it has been there long enough that the adhesive failed. Someone taped it back. Someone else taped it again. There are now three layers of tape. The note reads: Out of Order. Do Not Use. Repairs Pending. The machine still runs. It produces coffee. The coffee is not good. Fourteen people use it every day. None of them have reported it broken because none of them believe they are the person who should report it. This is not apathy. The fourteen people each care deeply. They simply do not know where to send the form. There is a form. It is on the intranet. The intranet page has been under maintenance since Tuesday. A different Tuesday.
